result:
The compressor coughs once and settles into a steady hum, a sound like a held note under everything else. The hose snakes along the curb, the steel wand cool in my palm. It is 3:04 a.m., when the city belongs to loading docks, raccoons, and us. We draw lines where the day will pretend it’s always been orderly.

The paint is the color of a promise—bright, immodest white that will be scuffed and dimmed before the week ends. If you’ve ever wondered why it flashes back at your headlights like a cat’s eye, it’s the glass in it. We feed the beads from a hopper and they fall like salt, disappearing into wetness, small spheres that bite the light and throw it home again. I like the sound they make, the hiss of the wand and the whispering pepper of beads, like rain that’s making up its mind.

There are rules to this, a geometry you wouldn’t believe. We don’t just eyeball a crosswalk. There are standards that tell me how long and wide a stripe should be for a street that carries buses, for a school zone, for the place where a new coffee shop will teach tired people to stop after months of rolling through. I used to think lines were bossy—stand here, go there, no, not now—but it turns out they’re shy. They only help if you look. They only help if they are present at all.

At this hour I take a piece of chalk and snap a line from curb to curb, a thin ghost that will be buried beneath paint before a delivery truck can rub it off. Sometimes I write notes in chalk I know will vanish: drink water, do it right, don’t hurry just because it’s cold. When I sweep the wand back and the paint goes down steady and sure, the chalk disappears under the new thing we’ve made, and I feel a strange twinge, like burying a secret that was always meant for compost.

You can tell something about a neighborhood by its crosswalks. In some places the lines are still there the next month, only a little smudged. In others, the tire bites are sharp as teeth and the white goes gray in a night. That isn’t always about money or care; sometimes it’s just the rhythm of trucks, the angle of sun, the way buses turn too tight because of a tree their drivers love. You learn the temperament of a block the way a bartender knows the afternoon regulars. The six-way where the bike lanes weave, the school corner with parents whose feet never stop dancing, the hill where water runs in winter and eats the paint away from the inside.

I’m not sentimental about it most of the time. Something goes down, something fades, we come back. I keep a calendar in my head and in a locker, both of them messy, dotted with notes from inspectors and complaints from people who remembered a better crosswalk ten years ago. But there are intersections that reach under your ribs. A few months ago, a woman came to the curb with her grandson in a dinosaur raincoat. The boy pressed the beg button with the solemn authority of a king. They waited. Traffic didn’t. No one broke the law, not exactly. But the white had gone to the ghost stage and the cars read the world as theirs. I told my foreman we should come back that night. There was no argument. There are things we can do nothing about, and there are four-inch stripes exactly nine inches apart.

This is the work: we lay out a ladder of paint and beads, and in the morning people step on each rung without thinking. A waitress with a keyring that sings. A guy who knows three city pigeons by face. A nurse whose shoes carry home the quiet from rooms you will never enter. I like to imagine the lines under their feet saying, easy now, we’ve got you. I don’t know if that’s corny or just true.

On the other side of the truck, Ernesto checks the bead flow and gives me a nod. He has the tuned ear of a drummer; he can hear when the pressure is wrong by the pitch of air escaping the nozzle. We trade places without words, the way people do when they share a task that requires both attention and boredom. There is so much boredom in doing something well. You cannot cheat a straight line. The stick in me that once made me want to be a painter—the other kind—finds satisfaction in the exactness of this. I used to draw birds on receipts between latte orders. Now the birds call back from a sycamore. It is the same gesture, the same approval of a steady hand.

People sometimes ask how long the lines last. If it rains before we finish, not long. If the sun hits at a steep angle all summer, less long. Thermoplastic lasts longer, but it requires heat and time and money they give us on some nights and not on others. Everything is conditional. There have been budget meetings; there have been years when we’ve stretched a can like a story, when we put down enough to be legal but not loud. You can feel your own frustration boiling up and then you lift the wand and lay a stripe that is exactly the width it should be because none of that is the driver’s fault or the first grader’s fault and the line, at least, can be perfect.

A skater rolls through the intersection at 3:31, hoodie up, eyes lowered. He glides like he is tied to the air itself. He glances down once at the bright ladder we’re making and gives a small, one-handed salute—thanks, or sorry, or nice line, who knows. I smile into the muffler of the truck and reach for another bag of beads. I think of how often we are in each other’s gratitude without seeing the faces attached to it. The city runs on plumbing and patience and little rituals like flicking your brights off half a block early. I like being one of the invisible hands.

By four, the corner has transformed. It is the same crosswalk, of course, same curb chips, same pothole that has started as a hairline and now fits a coin. But the white is loud, visible from half a block, telling night drivers the truth even if they would rather snooze through it. The hand on the signal flashes solid red. I could stand here all morning watching the first shift roll in over the paint, but we have three more corners and there’s a storm nosing its way in from the lake. If the rain comes, it will pockmark our work like lunar soil and you’ll see it forever, every flaw frozen under clear. Better to beat it.

There is a secret to the city that no brochure mentions. It is this: most of what keeps you safe, most of what makes your day smoother than it might have been, is the result of someone awake when you are dreaming, someone minding a detail they have learned to love. A properly tensioned elevator cable. A trash can lined before it smells. A bus seat wiped so the morning newspaper doesn’t print itself on your coat. Any of those people could tell you about lines.

We load the last of the cones. The compressor winds down, the night exhales, and a gull lifts off the roof where it has been pretending not to watch. Dawn isn’t here yet, but you can taste it in the air. We slow-roll the block so the paint has a few more minutes to cure. The radio crackles with a reminder about a school on the south side where the letters have almost vanished from SLOW. We’ll go there next. When we’re gone, the light will come up and the crosswalk will look a little too bright, like teeth in a new smile. Someone will step out, then another someone, and a driver will pause half a second longer than they would have yesterday.

It’s nothing. It’s a line. It’s everything.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
